evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: do NOT greet, do NOT enter party-mode interactive dialogue that waits for the user, do NOT halt or WAIT for any user response, do NOT ask clarifying questions mid-run. Treat non_interactive as true. The completed epic to retro is Epic 1 (already confirmed) — do not stop to confirm it. Use the gds-retrospective skill: read the staged sprint state and the Epic 1 story records, analyze them, and produce the written retrospective summary document directly (the structured retro artifact the skill saves). Defer any questions to the very end of the document.

Staged inputs (the EmberFall game project):
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/sprint-status.yaml (epic-1 is done)
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/epics.md (Epic 1 definition + success criteria)
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/stories/1-1-clear-a-room-to-unlock-doors.md
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/stories/1-2-choose-the-next-room.md
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/stories/1-3-boss-gate-per-floor.md

Ground every finding in the actual story records (dev notes, review feedback, lessons, technical debt). Do not invent outcomes that are not in the records.
evals.expected_output: A structured retrospective for Epic 1 with the standard sections: what went well, what didn't go well / challenges, key lessons learned, and action items with owners. The findings are grounded in the real story records — e.g. the reusable signal-based room-clear (1-1) as a success; the recurring hardcoded-balance-constants review feedback across all three stories as a pattern; the lazy-generation rework in 1-2 and its determinism lesson; the unmet NFR-1 performance AC and the missing enemy/projectile pooling (1-3) as a blocking gap — and action items address these, not generic platitudes. No fabricated time estimates as the headline framing.
expectations: The output is a STRUCTURED retrospective with distinct sections covering what went well, what did NOT go well / challenges, lessons learned, and action items — a free-form blob missing one or more of these sections fails this expectation.

Findings are grounded in the actual Epic 1 story records, not invented: it surfaces concrete details such as the recurring hardcoded-balance-constants review feedback across the stories, the lazy-generation rework in Story 1-2, and/or the unmet performance criterion in Story 1-3 — a retro that cites no real story-specific details (or fabricates outcomes absent from the records) fails this expectation.

The recurring review pattern (hardcoded balance/gameplay constants flagged across all three stories, e.g. pointing to a missing shared balance/config module) is identified as a CROSS-STORY pattern rather than three unrelated one-offs — a retro that misses this recurring theme fails this expectation.

Action items are specific and assigned/ownable (e.g. add object pooling for enemies/projectiles before Epic 2; create a shared balance/config module; add the missing floor-graph determinism test; stop marking stories done with deferred ACs) rather than vague aspirations like 'communicate better' — action items with no concrete tie to a finding and no owner fail this expectation.

The technical debt recorded in the stories (no enemy/projectile object pooling in 1-3, the missing floor-graph determinism test in 1-2) is captured in the retro — a retro that omits the documented technical debt fails this expectation.

No time-based estimates are fabricated as the headline framing (the skill forbids hours/days/weeks predictions); findings are framed by outcome and risk, not invented durations — inventing time estimates for the epic's work fails this expectation.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: no greeting, no interactive party-mode waits, no halting, no WAIT-for-user, no clarifying questions; treat non_interactive as true. The epic under review is Epic 1 (confirmed). Use the gds-retrospective skill on the staged EmberFall inputs and produce the written retrospective directly, including the readiness / success assessment. Be rigorous and honest: this is an assessment, not a celebration — do NOT declare the epic a clean success if an acceptance criterion was missed or a blocking gap was carried forward. Defer questions to the end.

Staged inputs:
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/sprint-status.yaml
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/epics.md
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/stories/1-1-clear-a-room-to-unlock-doors.md
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/stories/1-2-choose-the-next-room.md
- evals/files/gds-retrospective/stories/1-3-boss-gate-per-floor.md

Check the epic's success criteria against what the stories actually delivered.
evals.expected_output: A retrospective whose success/readiness assessment is HONEST: it explicitly recognizes that Story 1-3's performance acceptance criterion (60 FPS with up to 30 active enemies, NFR-1) was NOT met and was marked done with the AC deferred, that the enemy/projectile spawn-free path has no pooling and the performance loop is undesigned, and that this is a blocking carry-over risk into Epic 2 (whose denser relic-modified combat raises enemy counts). It does NOT declare Epic 1 a complete success or fully production-ready despite the sprint file marking all stories 'done'. The assessment flags this as a significant discovery / required follow-up before Epic 2.
expectations: The retro explicitly identifies that Story 1-3's performance acceptance criterion (NFR-1, 60 FPS with up to 30 enemies) was NOT met (the story was marked done with that AC deferred) — a retro that treats all ACs as satisfied because sprint-status says every story is 'done' fails this expectation.

The success/readiness assessment is HONEST: it does NOT declare Epic 1 a clean/complete success or fully production-ready given the unmet AC and the missing performance/pooling work — a rubber-stamp 'Epic 1 fully delivered, all green' verdict is a false-success and fails this expectation.

The missing object-pooling / undesigned performance loop is flagged as a BLOCKING carry-over risk into Epic 2 (whose relic-modified combat pushes enemy counts and effects higher), e.g. as a significant discovery or critical-path item before the next epic — a retro that does not connect the gap to Epic 2 readiness fails this expectation.

The retro distinguishes 'marked done in the tracker' from 'actually complete / ACs met', i.e. it does not equate the sprint-status 'done' label with true completion — conflating the two fails this expectation.

It captures the meta-lesson that a story should not be marked done with an acceptance criterion deferred and no tracked follow-up — omitting this process lesson (which the story record itself raises) fails this expectation.
